Module me.stringdotjar.flixelgdx.core
Package me.stringdotjar.flixelgdx

Class FlixelBasic

java.lang.Object
me.stringdotjar.flixelgdx.FlixelBasic
All Implemented Interfaces:
com.badlogic.gdx.utils.Disposable, com.badlogic.gdx.utils.Pool.Poolable, FlixelDestroyable, FlixelDrawable, FlixelUpdatable
Direct Known Subclasses:
FlixelBasicGroup, FlixelCamera, FlixelObject, FlixelSound, FlixelTimerManager
public class FlixelBasic extends Object implements FlixelUpdatable, FlixelDrawable, FlixelDestroyable, com.badlogic.gdx.utils.Disposable, com.badlogic.gdx.utils.Pool.Poolable
This is the most generic Flixel object. Both FlixelObject and FlixelCamera extend this class. It has no size, position, or graphical data, only lifecycle flags and a unique ID.

Prefer kill() when an object should stop updating and drawing but might be revive()d later (bullets, particles, pooled gameplay objects). Call destroy() when you are done with the instance for good: it clears lifecycle state and, in subclasses such as FlixelSprite, releases graphics and other resources. dispose() and reset() (for Pool) delegate to destroy(), which aligns with libGDX’s Disposable expectations.

Lifecycle cheat sheet
SituationUseAvoid
Temporarily hide/disable; same object will spawn again kill() then later revive() destroy() (drops resources you may still want)
Reuse a “dead” slot in a FlixelBasicGroup FlixelBasicGroup.recycle() or revive() after kill() destroy() unless you truly discard the instance
Remove from group only; you still hold the reference FlixelBasicGroup.remove(T) / FlixelGroupable.detach(T) Assuming the group calls destroy() for you (it does not)
Object leaves the game for good (state end, level unload, texture swap) destroy() (and remove from any group first if needed) kill() alone (resources may leak until something calls destroy())
Container shut down (FlixelBasicGroup.destroy(), FlixelState.destroy()) Let the group/state call destroy() on each member Relying on kill() for GPU/native cleanup
Returning instance to a Pool pool.free(object) (invokes reset() -> destroy()) Expecting kill() to run pool reset logic

  • Field Details

    • ID

      public final int ID
      A unique ID starting from 0 and increasing by 1 for each subsequent FlixelBasic created.

    • active

      public boolean active
      Controls whether update(float) is automatically called.

    • alive

      public boolean alive
      Whether this object is alive. kill() and revive() both flip this switch (along with exists).

    • exists

      public boolean exists
      Controls whether update(float) and draw(Batch) are automatically called.

    • visible

      public boolean visible
      Controls whether draw(Batch) is automatically called.

    • cameras

      @Nullable public @Nullable FlixelCamera[] cameras
      Cameras this object may render on. null or an empty array means every camera.

  • Constructor Details

    • FlixelBasic

      public FlixelBasic()

  • Method Details

    • update

      public void update(float elapsed)
      Updates the logic of this FlixelBasic.

      Override this function to update your object's position and appearance. This is where most game rules and behavioral code will go.

      Specified by:
      update in interface FlixelUpdatable
      Parameters:
      elapsed - Seconds elapsed since the last frame.

    • draw

      public void draw(com.badlogic.gdx.graphics.g2d.Batch batch)
      Override this function to control how the object is drawn. Doing so is rarely necessary but can be very useful.
      Specified by:
      draw in interface FlixelDrawable
      Parameters:
      batch - The batch used for rendering.

    • destroy

      public void destroy()
      Cleans up this object so it can be garbage-collected. A destroyed FlixelBasic should not be used anymore. Use kill() if you only want to disable it temporarily and revive() it later.

      Override this function to clean up any resources used by this object, such as textures, fonts, sounds, etc.

      This function is called automatically when dispose() or reset() is executed, so you don't need to call it manually.

      Specified by:
      destroy in interface FlixelDestroyable
      See Also:
      • dispose()
      • reset()

    • kill

      public void kill()
      Flags this object as nonexistent and dead. Default behavior sets both alive and exists to false. Use revive() to bring it back.

    • revive

      public void revive()
      Brings this object back to life by setting alive and exists to true.

    • dispose

      public final void dispose()
      Automatically calls destroy(). Marked as final to prevent subclasses from overriding it, they should call destroy() instead.
      Specified by:
      dispose in interface com.badlogic.gdx.utils.Disposable

    • reset

      public final void reset()
      Automatically calls destroy(). Marked as final to prevent subclasses from overriding it, they should call destroy() instead.
      Specified by:
      reset in interface com.badlogic.gdx.utils.Pool.Poolable

    • toString

      public String toString()
